IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important problem—modeling contextual dependencies for multimodal utterance-level sentiment—and demonstrates substantial empirical gains (claimed 5–10% improvement over prior state of the art) across standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP). Its hierarchical fusion and bidirectional contextual LSTM variants highlight a clear methodological advance that will likely change how researchers model temporal context in multimodal fusion and spur follow-up work (e.g., context-aware attention). Limitations noted (cross-dataset language issues, noise sensitivity) indicate realistic boundaries but do not undermine the broader impact or likelihood that others will build on these ideas. | B(4): The paper targets an important problem—utterance-level multimodal sentiment/emotion recognition—by explicitly modeling contextual dependencies across utterances and proposing a hierarchical fusion LSTM framework. It reports consistent 5–10% improvements over prior state-of-the-art on standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) and shows bidirectional context (bc-LSTM) yields further gains, indicating that context modeling materially changes performance. The hierarchical per-modality then cross-modal contextual fusion is a clear methodological contribution that is likely to influence subsequent work (e.g., context-aware attention) and be adopted or extended by researchers. Given these dataset-backed gains and the generality of the approach, the work has strong impact potential though it is not necessarily transformative across all of AI.

Its hierarchical fusion and bidirectional contextual LSTM variants highlight a clear methodological advance that will likely change how researchers model temporal context in multimodal fusion and spur follow-up work (e.g., context-aware attention). Limitations noted (cross-dataset language issues, noise sensitivity) indicate realistic boundaries but do not undermine the broader impact or likelihood that others will build on these ideas.

Given these dataset-backed gains and the generality of the approach, the work has strong impact potential though it is not necessarily transformative across all of AI.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): Yes — the paper addresses multimodal sentiment analysis using contextual LSTM architectures and hierarchical fusion, which squarely fits the scope of top NLP/ML venues. It evaluates on standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP), uses expected baselines and metrics (uni‑SVM, prior SOTA by Poria et al. and Rozgic et al., accuracy), and compares contextual vs. non‑contextual fusion variants, making it relevant to both the NLP and ML research communities. Overall the topic, audience, problem formulation, methods, and positioning are appropriate for a top conference. | B(5): Yes — the paper addresses multimodal sentiment analysis using contextual LSTM architectures and hierarchical fusion, which squarely fits the interests of top NLP/ML venues working on sequence modeling, representation learning, and multimodal understanding. It uses standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP), compares to relevant baselines (uni-SVM, Poria et al., Rozgic et al.), reports common metrics (accuracy), and follows expected experimental practice. The problem formulation (utterance-level contextual modeling) and methodological choices (text-/audio-/video feature extraction followed by LSTM-based contextual and fusion models) are appropriate for the venue’s audience and research community.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes a hierarchical contextual LSTM framework (per-modality contextual LSTMs followed by a second-level contextual LSTM for fusion) and shows bidirectional context helps, which is a clear, concrete contribution relative to the cited MKL and SVM-tree baselines. However, the approach largely combines established components (text-CNN, openSMILE, 3D-CNN feature extractors and LSTMs for sequence modeling) and represents an incremental architectural extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The authors do clearly differentiate their method and demonstrate empirical gains, so the novelty is moderate rather than minimal. | B(3): The paper applies contextual LSTM variants (sc-LSTM, bc-LSTM, h-LSTM) and a hierarchical fusion (per-modality contextual LSTMs followed by a second-level contextual LSTM) to multimodal sentiment, showing 5–10% gains over prior work. However, the core components (text-CNN, openSMILE, 3D-CNN feature extractors; LSTMs; concatenation/hierarchical fusion) are existing techniques and the contribution is primarily an application/combination rather than a fundamentally new model or formulation. The authors do clearly differentiate and empirically compare to prior methods (Poria et al., Rozgic et al.), but the novelty appears incremental rather than highly original.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments cover three standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) and compare multiple architectural variants (sc-LSTM, bc-LSTM, h-LSTM) as well as hierarchical vs. non‑hierarchical fusion, which serves as useful ablation to support the main claims. Related work is described as adequate and concrete failure cases and dataset-specific limitations (language mismatch, noisy audio/faces, poor neutral-class performance) are explicitly discussed. Hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility, but code is not mentioned and some analyses (e.g., attention remedy experiments or more extensive sensitivity studies) are missing, so the evaluation is thorough but with minor gaps. | B(4): The experiments are solid: evaluations on three standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) and comparisons to uni-SVM and prior methods (Poria et al., Rozgic et al.) support the main claims, and reported 5–10% improvements are convincing. Appropriate ablations/sensitivity analyses are present (comparison of fusion strategies, sc-LSTM vs bc-LSTM vs h-LSTM, and bidirectional vs unidirectional), and related work coverage is described as adequate. The paper discusses failure cases and dataset-specific limitations (face/audio noise, language mismatch MOSI→MOUD, poor neutral predictions) and reports hyperparameters, but code is not mentioned and some reproducibility/generalization analyses (e.g., cross-language mitigation or attention ablations) are missing, hence not fully exhaustive.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments cover three standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) and compare multiple architectural variants (sc-LSTM, bc-LSTM, h-LSTM) as well as hierarchical vs. non‑hierarchical fusion, which serves as useful ablation to support the main claims. Related work is described as adequate and concrete failure cases and dataset-specific limitations (language mismatch, noisy audio/faces, poor neutral-class performance) are explicitly discussed. Hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility, but code is not mentioned and some analyses (e.g., attention remedy experiments or more extensive sensitivity studies) are missing, so the evaluation is thorough but with minor gaps.

Appropriate ablations/sensitivity analyses are present (comparison of fusion strategies, sc-LSTM vs bc-LSTM vs h-LSTM, and bidirectional vs unidirectional), and related work coverage is described as adequate. The paper discusses failure cases and dataset-specific limitations (face/audio noise, language mismatch MOSI→MOUD, poor neutral predictions) and reports hyperparameters, but code is not mentioned and some reproducibility/generalization analyses (e.g., cross-language mitigation or attention ablations) are missing, hence not fully exhaustive.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates on standard datasets (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) and compares against a uni-SVM baseline and prior state-of-the-art methods (Poria et al. 2015; Rozgic et al. 2012) using the same accuracy metric, and it analyzes ablations between LSTM variants and fusion strategies. It also discusses limitations and cross-dataset issues, indicating fair experimental conditions. However, the summary suggests only a small set of baselines (classic SVM/MKL/SVM-tree methods) were used, so there may be minor omissions of more recent neural baselines despite overall adequate related-work coverage. | B(4): The paper compares to sensible and relevant baselines (uni-SVM, Poria et al. 2015 MKL, Rozgic et al. 2012) on standard benchmarks (MOSI, MOUD, IEMOCAP) using the same evaluation metric (accuracy), and reports hyperparameters with adequate related-work coverage, indicating generally fair comparisons. However, the summary suggests claims of a 5–10% improvement over prior state of the art without indicating inclusion of potentially newer deep-learning baselines, and it does not clearly state when prior methods perform comparably or better in specific cases. Therefore the comparisons are good overall but may omit some recent competing approaches or detailed acknowledgment of cases where prior work matches performance.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments on MOSI, MOUD and IEMOCAP against sensible baselines (uni‑SVM and cited prior work), with hyperparameters reported and reported gains (2–5% unimodal, 5–10% overall). There are minor methodological caveats: no mention of statistical significance testing, a cross‑dataset language mismatch (MOSI→MOUD) that limits generalization claims, and the use of bidirectional context (bc‑LSTM) may not reflect online/causal settings. No mathematical derivations of concern are needed for the proposed LSTM architectures and none are evidently incorrect. | B(4): The main claims are supported by empirical evidence: experiments on MOSI, MOUD and IEMOCAP compare sc-LSTM, bc-LSTM and h-LSTM to uni-SVM and prior work (Poria et al., Rozgic et al.) and report consistent improvements (2–10%), with bc-LSTM outperforming unidirectional variants. Mathematical elements are standard LSTM architectures and appear complete for the scope; hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, code release, or detailed ablations controlling for feature-extractor choices, and there are acknowledged cross-dataset and modality limitations, so a few methodological/replicability issues remain but do not overturn the main experimental conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments on MOSI, MOUD and IEMOCAP against sensible baselines (uni‑SVM and cited prior work), with hyperparameters reported and reported gains (2–5% unimodal, 5–10% overall). There are minor methodological caveats: no mention of statistical significance testing, a cross‑dataset language mismatch (MOSI→MOUD) that limits generalization claims, and the use of bidirectional context (bc‑LSTM) may not reflect online/causal settings. No mathematical derivations of concern are needed for the proposed LSTM architectures and none are evidently incorrect.

Mathematical elements are standard LSTM architectures and appear complete for the scope; hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, code release, or detailed ablations controlling for feature-extractor choices, and there are acknowledged cross-dataset and modality limitations, so a few methodological/replicability issues remain but do not overturn the main experimental conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized and the main contributions are stated early: the contextual LSTM framework, hierarchical fusion design, and comparisons against uni-SVM and prior work are clearly described, with methodology (text-CNN, openSMILE, 3D-CNN feeding into LSTM variants) and experiments on MOSI, MOUD, and IEMOCAP reported. Writing quality is noted as good and hyperparameters and related work coverage are provided, which supports clarity. Minor presentation gaps remain in the summary (no mention of figures/tables effectiveness or whether notation is explicitly defined, and code availability is not stated), so I rate it a strong but not perfect 4. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear methodology and experiment structure (methodology, datasets MOSI/MOUD/IEMOCAP, baselines, metrics) indicating a logical flow. The main contributions are stated early and explicitly (contextual LSTM variants, hierarchical fusion, comparisons to prior work). Notation and presentation seem consistent and readable (hyperparameters reported, related work adequate, writing quality: good), though the summary does not mention figures/tables explicitly, leaving minor uncertainty about their effectiveness.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized and the main contributions are stated early: the contextual LSTM framework, hierarchical fusion design, and comparisons against uni-SVM and prior work are clearly described, with methodology (text-CNN, openSMILE, 3D-CNN feeding into LSTM variants) and experiments on MOSI, MOUD, and IEMOCAP reported. Writing quality is noted as good and hyperparameters and related work coverage are provided, which supports clarity. Minor presentation gaps remain in the summary (no mention of figures/tables effectiveness or whether notation is explicitly defined, and code availability is not stated), so I rate it a strong but not perfect 4.

Notation and presentation seem consistent and readable (hyperparameters reported, related work adequate, writing quality: good), though the summary does not mention figures/tables explicitly, leaving minor uncertainty about their effectiveness.

main_claims: Proposes a contextual LSTM framework that models dependencies among utterances in a video to produce context-sensitive unimodal and multimodal features for utterance-level sentiment/emotion classification.

A hierarchical fusion framework that first produces context-sensitive unimodal features via per-modality LSTMs and then fuses them with a second-level contextual LSTM outperforms a non-hierarchical fusion and context-independent baselines.

LSTM-based variants (sc-LSTM, bc-LSTM, h-LSTM) outperform a uni-SVM baseline by 2–5% and outperform prior state-of-the-art methods (Poria et al., Rozgic et al.) by a significant margin, with an overall claimed 5–10% improvement over the state of the art.

Bidirectional contextual LSTM (bc-LSTM), which has access to both preceding and following utterances, performs consistently better than the unidirectional variant (sc-LSTM); modeling contextual dependencies is key to improved performance.
_summary.methodology: Extract context-independent unimodal features using text-CNN (text), openSMILE (audio), and 3D-CNN (video), then feed these into contextual LSTM networks (variants sc-LSTM, bc-LSTM, h-LSTM) and perform multimodal fusion either by concatenation (non-hierarchical) or via a two-level hierarchical fusion where per-modality contextual features are combined by a second-level contextual LSTM.
baselines: Poria et al. (2015) multiple kernel learning (MKL) method
Rozgic et al. (2012b) method (SVM trees for fusion, as cited)
limitations: Poor predictions when face is hard to recognize or audio is noisy.
Bias toward surrounding utterances can cause errors when sentiment is weak and non-contextual; authors suggest a context-aware attention mechanism as a remedy.
Cross-dataset/generalization issues for audio and textual modalities in MOSI→MOUD due to language mismatch (MOUD in Spanish, MOSI in English).
Unimodal and multimodal classifiers performed poorly on neutral utterances in IEMOCAP; textual translation noise (Spanish→English) harmed MOUD textual performance.
